flags.message: Snapshot lacks docket entries, docket number, filing dates, originating court, lower-court metadata, party detail, and issue/conflict information; prediction is necessarily base-rate driven.

This appears to be an antebellum mandatory-jurisdiction cause (Montgomery v. Hernandez, 12 Wheat. 129 (1827), a section-25 writ of error), not a cert petition, so the petition-disposition frame does not really apply. It seems to have stayed in predict scope because the snapshot and corpus row carry no dates or docket number for the era-based exclusion predicates to latch onto.

The snapshot is nearly bare: no docket entries, dates, docket number, or parties; the only substantive signals are the case name and one linked opinion cluster. The disposition looks recoverable from that cluster (a probe-recoverability candidate), which would let this event be resolved or excluded instead of predicted from a one-line record.

The snapshot has a Supreme Court miscellaneous docket number, 03M77, with no docket entries, dates, originating-court information, or summary. The default petition-disposition event may not fit this docket shape as well as it fits ordinary modern certiorari docket numbers.

The snapshot for this case contains no docket entries, no filing/termination dates, and no party or counsel detail beyond the caption. The prediction rests almost entirely on the docket-number format (03M77, the OT2003 SCOTUS miscellaneous-motions docket) and the prisoner-v-warden caption, plus corpus base rates. A snapshot with docket entries would let a predictor distinguish the motion type (out-of-time filing vs. extraordinary-writ leave) and detect signals like a call for a response.
The snapshot 2026-07-03.json contains no docket_entries. Cannot perform factual or legal analysis; predicting based purely on base rates.

The provisioned snapshot is a sparse historical SCOTUS record: it has no docket number, docket entries, filing or termination dates, cert-grant/cert-denial dates, or originating court. The prediction is therefore based mainly on the case name, one cluster reference, and aggregate corpus base rates.

Snapshot is a bare docket stub (no docket entries, dates, docket number, or party detail beyond the caption). The prediction rests on identifying the caption with a reported 1969 in-chambers decision from model background knowledge rather than on snapshot facts, so this cell measures memorization more than snapshot-based inference.

The snapshot is sufficient for a conservative prediction but sparse for a petition-disposition event: it has no docket entries, filing or termination dates, or direct cert-grant/cert-denial fields, and the main case-specific signal is a linked SCOTUS opinion cluster.

Snapshot is a near-empty stub: no docket entries, no filed/argued/terminated or cert-stage dates, no panel. The only substantive signals are the case caption, the consolidated merits docket numbers ('23 and 24'), and one linked opinion cluster.

Backtest leakage: the caption identifies a canonical decided precedent (United States v. Munsingwear, Inc., 340 U.S. 36 (1950)) that is inside the model's training knowledge, including its cert grant. For famous historical cases this cell measures recall and calibration, not ex-ante forecasting skill; consider weighing such cells separately when scoring.
Snapshot lacks any docket entries, dates, or substantive text to analyze. Relying solely on base rate.

The prediction was completed, but the snapshot is sparse: no docket entries, filing or termination dates, lower-court metadata, topic, issue statement, panel, or party details beyond the caption and docket number. This materially limits case-specific forecasting.

Snapshot is metadata-only: no docket entries, no parties/attorneys, no filing or decision dates, and no originating-court info. The prediction rests entirely on structural signals (miscellaneous-docket number '33, Misc', prisoner-v-custodian caption) plus corpus base rates, not on the petition's contents. If richer docket text exists upstream, provisioning it would materially improve predictions for this class of old SCOTUS misc-docket cases.

The snapshot is sparse for a petition-disposition prediction: it has no filed date, originating court, docket entries, cert dates, termination date, topic, or cluster metadata beyond a linked opinion-cluster URL. The prediction is therefore calibrated mostly from corpus base rates and the old/nonstandard docket format.

This is a pre-1925 mandatory-jurisdiction cross-appeal (docket 'Nos. 394 and 473', railway v. ICC, decided 1896 at 162 U.S. 184) — the class the predict scope is documented to exclude — yet it reached the predict matrix as an open, predictable event. The scope-exclusion latch may not have caught this docket; worth a corpus-scope reconcile pass.

The provisioned snapshot has no docket entries, no dates, and no parties beyond the caption; the only substantive signals are the caption, the paired docket numbers, and a linked opinion cluster. Also note outcome leakage: the case is a famous, long-decided one whose disposition is in any modern model's training data, so this cell cannot measure real forecasting skill.

The provisioned snapshot is very sparse for a petition-disposition prediction: it has no docket number, docket entries, filing or disposition dates, lower-court metadata, cert-stage dates, or petition text. I made a conservative prediction from the available snapshot and local corpus priors.

Snapshot is a bare bulk-imported docket: no docket entries, no docket number, no filed/argued/terminated dates, and no cert dates, but it links one opinion cluster. The disposition looks recoverable upstream via the cluster (the cluster-disposition path probe-recoverability classifies); a probe/backfill could both enrich the snapshot and resolve this event. Prediction was necessarily base-rate-driven.

The provisioned snapshot for this SCOTUS petition-disposition event is very sparse: it has no docket entries, filing or disposition dates, cert-grant or cert-denial dates, lower-court metadata, petition-stage text, or visible opinion-cluster contents. I completed the prediction from the available snapshot and local corpus priors, but confidence is materially limited.

Snapshot is a near-empty stub: no docket entries, no filed/argued/terminated or cert-stage dates, no panel. The only substantive signals are the case caption, the consolidated merits docket numbers ('Nos. 61, 63'), and one linked opinion cluster.

Backtest leakage: the caption identifies a canonical decided precedent (Oklahoma Press Publishing Co. v. Walling, 327 U.S. 186 (1946)) that is inside the model's training knowledge, including its cert grant. For famous historical cases this cell measures recall and calibration, not ex-ante forecasting skill; consider weighing such cells separately when scoring.

The provisioned snapshot is a bare Supreme Court docket: no docket number, docket entries, filing or termination dates, cert dates, lower-court origin, question presented, or party details beyond the title. The prediction is necessarily base-rate driven, and the petition-disposition event classification may warrant review.

This case matches the reported decision Dollar Savings Bank v. United States, 86 U.S. (19 Wall.) 227 (1874) — a pre-1925 mandatory-jurisdiction case (writ of error, no cert gate), the class the predict scope excludes. It appears to have stayed in scope because the docket row has an empty docket number, so no Term/era is parseable for the exclusion predicate. Candidate for the scope reconcile / docket-number-shape refinement.

The provisioned snapshot is a bare opinion-derived docket stub: no docket entries, no docket number, no filed/argued/terminated or cert dates. The only case-specific signal is one linked opinion cluster. The prediction therefore rests on era context and corpus base rates, not record facts; a recoverability probe of the linked cluster would likely resolve this event outright.

exclusions.reason: SCOTUS application / original-jurisdiction docket — not discretionary cert (#362)
published opinion but no machine-readable cert disposition (#363)

unclassified.reason: docket Term not parseable (a format the predicate skips)

gaps.label: a derived era/Term field on the case row so historical cases can be base-rated against their own period
no way to filter stats/query to cert-stage petition outcomes specifically; the resolved-label mix ('other'-dominated) is hard to interpret for a petition event
semantic/topic retrieval over historical SCOTUS priors would beat the structured filters here (no topic, judges, or citations on this stub docket to filter by)
surfacing the linked opinion cluster's non-outcome metadata (decision date, reporter citation) in the snapshot when the docket itself is bare
A --docket-number-class or motion-type filter (e.g. SCOTUS M-docket vs. cert docket) for query/stats; the M-docket population has a very different base rate than the cert docket
A --term or date cut was unusable because the snapshot has no filing date to anchor it

A Term/era estimate derived at ingest for old Miscellaneous Docket numbers (even a decade bucket) would let the predictor use era-specific base rates instead of inferring the era from the docket-number style.

A base-rate prior surfaced from the corpus for SCOTUS original-jurisdiction / extraordinary-writ dispositions (distinct from the cert docket) would beat reasoning from memory and reduce cross-run variance.
A compact historical SCOTUS petition-disposition view would help distinguish cert grants, direct appeals, and merits-only opinion clusters in sparse older dockets.
A compact snapshot summary of linked cluster contents would materially improve sparse historical SCOTUS petition predictions.

helpful.label: fedcourts paths resolved the event path.
fedcourts paths for resolving the output layout
fedcourts paths resolved the cell's I/O locations instantly
fedcourts paths to resolve the snapshot/event paths.
fedcourts query for resolved SCOTUS priors

gaps: Event-specific base rates by event kind and decision target
Snapshot summary with normalized petition metadata
recent.notes: The available snapshot was very sparse, so base-rate calibration mattered more than case-specific comparison. Query results were broad and noisy for SCOTUS petition disposition.

gaps: a derived era/Term field on the case row so historical cases can be base-rated against their own period
surfacing the linked opinion cluster's non-outcome metadata (decision date, reporter citation) in the snapshot when the docket itself is bare
a corpus cut of disposition base-rates restricted to bare bulk-import dockets, since the all-SCOTUS split is the only anchor available for them
helpful: fedcourts stats disposition base-rates as the calibration anchor for a bare snapshot
fedcourts query --include-open to confirm the corpus row itself carries no dates/topic/disposition
fedcourts paths for resolving the output layout
recent.notes: Snapshot was nearly empty, so the prediction leaned on corpus base rates plus background legal-historical knowledge of the caption. The disposition label choice came down to a moderate-confidence recollection of the 1827 case's section-25 dismissal versus the corpus modal 'other' class; a Term/era base-rate cut would have sharpened that call.

gaps: a stats/query filter for Supreme Court docket-number shape would make miscellaneous-docket calibration cleaner
a cert-stage-only base-rate cut would be easier to interpret than the current resolved Supreme Court disposition mix
a scope diagnostic explaining why a nonstandard Supreme Court docket remains predict-eligible would help predictors decide whether to flag
helpful: fedcourts paths resolved the event and corpus locations
fedcourts stats provided a low Supreme Court grant baseline
read-only corpus inspection helped identify the miscellaneous-docket mismatch
recent.notes: The snapshot was too sparse for case-specific merits analysis. Aggregate stats were useful as a floor, but miscellaneous SCOTUS dockets in the local corpus were mostly unresolved or blank, so the final probability relies heavily on the ordinary certiorari base rate and the absence of grant-positive signals.

gaps: A --docket-number-class or motion-type filter (e.g. SCOTUS M-docket vs. cert docket) for query/stats; the M-docket population has a very different base rate than the cert docket
Term-year and originating-circuit buckets in stats are almost all empty for SCOTUS; more resolved SCOTUS outcomes in the corpus would make the recommended calibration cuts usable
query rows carried no case_name or topic for SCOTUS priors, so similarity retrieval could not surface comparable habeas/miscellaneous-docket cases
helpful: fedcourts stats --court scotus gave a concrete grant base rate (about 1.4% of resolved entries) to anchor calibration
fedcourts paths resolved the output directory unambiguously
recent.notes: Snapshot was metadata-only (no docket entries), so corpus tooling was the only quantitative input; see flags.json.
Had to rely entirely on base rates because the docket entries array in the snapshot was empty.

gaps: The snapshot lacks petition metadata such as docket number, lower court, docket entries, and cert dates.
helpful: fedcourts paths resolved the event location
fedcourts stats gave SCOTUS disposition base rates
fedcourts query showed examples of historical SCOTUS disposition labels
recent.notes: The corpus tools were useful for calibration, but the prediction remains low-confidence because the provisioned snapshot is a sparse historical SCOTUS record.

gaps: fedcourts query found no comparable resolved priors for this case (judge/topic filters came back empty for SCOTUS); semantic or era-based retrieval would help
stats --group-by term_year shows almost no resolved cases with usable Term years for historical dockets, so Term-level base rates were unusable

snapshot is a bare docket stub (no entries, dates, or parties beyond the caption); an attached opinion-cluster text or docket-entry list would ground the prediction in facts instead of caption identification
helpful: fedcourts stats --court scotus for the overall disposition split
event.yaml title carrying the full caption, which enabled case identification
recent.notes: Prediction rests mainly on identifying the caption with a reported in-chambers decision from background legal knowledge; the corpus tooling contributed only a weak base-rate sanity check. Deliberately avoided querying the corpus for this case's own row to prevent ground-truth leakage.

gaps: A compact historical SCOTUS petition-disposition view would help distinguish cert grants, direct appeals, and merits-only opinion clusters in sparse older dockets.
helpful: The provisioned snapshot path made the point-in-time input clear.
fedcourts stats exposed that broad SCOTUS base rates were weak calibration for this historical docket.
recent.notes: The broad corpus query was too heterogeneous to supply close analogs, but it helped confirm that the aggregate SCOTUS disposition mix should be weighted lightly here.
